name: Xylella fastidiosa (olive quick decline syndrome)
treatment: No known cure. If confirmed, mandatory uprooting under regional regulations; monitor and control the insect vector (meadow spittlebug); report to the Regional Plant Health Observatory (Osservatorio Fitosanitario Regionale).
treatmentCaveats: This is a regulated quarantine pathogen in Puglia; do not attempt unauthorized treatment or movement of plant material from a suspect tree — all action must follow official containment protocols regardless of season or weather.

name: Olive fruit fly (Bactrocera oleae)
treatment: Install chromotropic traps for monitoring; apply protein bait sprays or copper-based treatments if infestation is high; harvest early if infestation is severe.
careAdjustments: Bait sprays work by attraction, so avoid placing bait stations near beneficial-insect foraging areas where possible; after any spray, respect the product label's pre-harvest interval before picking treated fruit.
treatmentCaveats: Avoid spraying during hot midday hours, when bait dries out too quickly to remain attractive; early morning or evening application is generally more effective and safer for pollinators.

name: Black scale (Saissetia oleae)
symptoms: small brown bumps on twigs and leaves
treatment: Prune to improve air circulation; introduce natural predators such as parasitic wasps; apply horticultural oil or a targeted insecticide in case of heavy infestation.
careAdjustments: After an oil spray, avoid stressing the tree with drought or waterlogging for the following days, and avoid applying sulfur within a few weeks of the oil treatment (the combination increases phytotoxicity risk).
treatmentCaveats: Avoid horticultural oil application above roughly 30-32 degrees Celsius or on drought-stressed trees, as both conditions significantly raise the risk of leaf scorch/phytotoxicity.

name: Peacock spot (Spilocaea oleagina)
symptoms: yellow-brown spots on upper leaf surface
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicide in autumn and spring; improve canopy ventilation through pruning; remove fallen infected leaves.
careAdjustments: After copper application, avoid overhead irrigation for a day or two so the deposit adheres and isn't prematurely washed off the leaf surface.
treatmentCaveats: Standard timing already targets autumn and pre-flowering spring windows; avoid spraying during flowering itself to protect pollinators, and avoid application right before prolonged rain, which both washes off the fungicide and favors further spread of the fungus.

name: Verticillium wilt (Verticillium dahliae)
treatment: No effective chemical cure; remove and destroy affected branches; avoid rotating with susceptible crops such as cotton or tomato; ensure balanced irrigation and avoid water stress.
careAdjustments: Disinfect pruning tools between cuts and between trees to reduce mechanical spread of the fungus; avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which can worsen symptom expression on already-infected trees.

name: Powdery mildew on grapevine (Erysiphe necator)
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicide treatments; improve canopy airflow through leaf removal; monitor humidity levels.
careAdjustments: After a sulfur application, avoid additional plant stress such as drought during the following days.
treatmentCaveats: Do not apply sulfur when air temperature is above roughly 30-32 degrees Celsius, since this substantially increases the risk of phytotoxic leaf and berry scorch; also avoid applying sulfur within about 2-3 weeks of an oil-based spray, as the combination is phytotoxic.

name: Downy mildew on grapevine (Plasmopara viticola)
symptoms: yellow oily spots on upper leaf surface
white downy mold on leaf underside
treatment: Apply copper-based or systemic fungicide treatments preventively before rain events; ensure good vineyard drainage and canopy ventilation.
careAdjustments: After copper application, avoid unnecessary overhead irrigation for a few days to prevent premature wash-off and to avoid extending leaf wetness that favors further fungal spread.
treatmentCaveats: Repeated seasonal copper use contributes to soil copper accumulation; EU Regulation 2018/1981 caps cumulative copper application at 28 kg metallic copper/ha over 7 years (a 4 kg/ha/year average, with a smoothing mechanism for difficult years) — avoid exceeding this regardless of disease pressure.

name: Citrus tristeza virus (CTV)
treatment: No cure once infected; use certified virus-free nursery stock; control aphid vectors such as the brown citrus aphid; remove and destroy infected trees to prevent spread.
careAdjustments: When removing infected material, disinfect pruning or grafting tools between trees to avoid mechanical transmission, even though the main spread route is aphid vectors and infected budwood.
treatmentCaveats: Never propagate or graft from a tree of uncertain virus status, regardless of season.

name: Cottony cushion scale (Icerya purchasi)
symptoms: white cottony masses on citrus branches
treatment: Introduce the natural predator Rodolia cardinalis (vedalia beetle); apply horticultural oil in case of heavy infestation; prune affected branches.
careAdjustments: Broad-spectrum insecticide use is incompatible with an established Rodolia cardinalis population in the same period, since it will also kill the beneficial predator; avoid combining oil with sulfur treatments in a short window due to phytotoxicity risk.
treatmentCaveats: Avoid oil spraying during citrus flowering to limit pollinator exposure, and avoid application in hot, sunny, drought-stressed conditions, which raise phytotoxicity risk.

name: Citrus leafminer (Phyllocnistis citrella)
symptoms: silvery serpentine trails on citrus leaves
treatment: Prune and destroy affected shoots; encourage natural parasitoid wasps; avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which stimulates the new flush growth the pest prefers.
careAdjustments: After pruning, avoid practices that stimulate a fresh flush right away (e.g. heavy nitrogen feeding or aggressive pruning), since new tender leaves are what attract the pest.
treatmentCaveats: Pruning affected shoots is most useful while the flush is young and actively being mined; once leaves harden, the mines are mostly cosmetic and pruning provides little practical benefit.

name: Fig rust (Cerotelium fici)
symptoms: orange-brown pustules on underside of fig leaves
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicide at first symptoms; remove and destroy fallen infected leaves; improve air circulation through pruning.
careAdjustments: After copper application, avoid overhead irrigation for a day or two to let the deposit set and to limit leaf wetness duration.
treatmentCaveats: Avoid applying copper fungicide in full midday sun or high heat, which increases risk of leaf phytotoxicity; if fruit is close to ripening, check the product label's pre-harvest interval before spraying.

name: Fig mosaic virus
symptoms: mottled yellow-green mosaic pattern on fig leaves
treatment: No cure available; propagate only from certified virus-free planting material; control eriophyid mite vectors; remove severely affected trees.
careAdjustments: When removing affected material, disinfect pruning tools between plants, and never take propagation cuttings from a symptomatic tree.

name: Almond leaf scorch (Xylella fastidiosa)
symptoms: marginal leaf scorch on almond leaves
treatment: No known cure; remove and destroy infected trees under regional Xylella containment protocols; control leafhopper and spittlebug vectors; report to the Regional Plant Health Observatory.
treatmentCaveats: Same as for the olive strain: this is a regulated quarantine pathogen; do not attempt unauthorized treatment or movement of plant material — follow official protocols regardless of season.

name: Brown rot (Monilinia laxa)
treatment: Apply fungicide treatments at bloom stage; prune out and destroy mummified fruit and blighted twigs; improve canopy ventilation.
careAdjustments: After a bloom-time fungicide application, avoid overhead irrigation for the following days to preserve the deposit and avoid extending petal wetness, which favors infection.
treatmentCaveats: Bloom-stage sprays should avoid peak flower-opening/pollinator-activity periods where possible (favor early morning or evening application) to reduce pollinator exposure; avoid spraying right before rain, since blossom blight risk is highest during wet, mild bloom-time weather and the fungicide would wash off before taking effect.
